summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while defeating SOPA and PIPA is necessary, it's not sufficient to protect the internet long-term. He explains how these bills would effectively hide websites from the internet rather than targeting actual piracy, making them dangerous to the open web. The post celebrates the successful grassroots opposition, particularly Wikipedia's blackout that brought mainstream attention to the issue. However, Atwood pivots to the deeper problem: the systemic corruption of money in politics that allowed such legislation to advance in the first place. Citing Lawrence Lessig, he argues that until campaign finance and lobbying reform is addressed, the tech community will face an endless stream of similar threats.
key_points: SOPA/PIPA would make websites effectively invisible by forcing maps, directories, payment processors, and advertisers to block them - a wildly disproportionate response to piracy
The bills advanced so far because average people don't understand the internet, voters ignore government until personally affected, and entertainment industry lobbyists pushed hard
Wikipedia's full blackout was the turning point that transformed this from a 'geek ghetto' issue into mainstream awareness
Defeating individual bills is insufficient - the root cause is money corrupting Congress through lobbying
Lawrence Lessig's framework: we will 'never (as in not ever)' win internet freedom battles until we address systemic political corruption
money_quotes: These bills are so rife with potential for abuse and misuse, so clearly dangerous to the very fabric of the Internet, that frankly I have a hard time getting worked up about them.
The Internet is under constant siege by large companies, and will be for the foreseeable future.
There's no better way to raise awareness of the danger these bills pose than blacklisting one of the greatest resources the Internet has ever produced.
Instead of celebrating, let's take this time to reflect and ask ourselves a deeper question: how is it that these dangerous bills came to exist in the first place?
We will never (as in not ever) win the war you care about until we win the war against this corruption of our Republic.
Please also join us in attacking the far more pernicious problem of lobbyist money subtly corrupting our government.
If we don't deal with that, we will never stop fighting bills like SOPA and PIPA.
key_insight: Winning individual battles against bad internet legislation is meaningless if we don't address the systemic corruption of money in politics that generates these threats in the first place.
